content:
The mistakes were not about forgetting hooks. They were about putting responsibility in the wrong place.

12 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

5 React Mistakes I Stopped MakingMost React mistakes do not feel like mistakes when you make them.
They feel practical.
You add a state because the component needs to remember something. You add an effect because data needs to load. You pass props down because a child needs a value. You create a reusable component because two screens look similar. You move logic into a hook because the file is getting long. You add a condition in JSX because the UI needs to change.
None of that feels wrong in the moment.
The feature works. The page renders. The pull request passes review. The user can click the button, submit the form, filter the table, open the drawer, and move on.
Then the app grows.
The state starts drifting. The effect runs more often than expected. The reusable component becomes impossible to use without five flags. The hook hides too much behavior. The table rerenders when unrelated filters change. The form submits stale values. The export does not match what the user sees. The UI condition that looked harmless becomes a business rule copied across multiple files.
